answers.field_label: Why Are You Interested In Serving On The Pennsylvania Prek Counts Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program Advisory Committee
answers.value:
I am interested in serving on the Pennsylvania PreK Counts/Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program Advisory Committee because my entire professional life and much of my personal life have centered on improving access to high‑quality early childhood education for children and families who are often overlooked. I have spent the last 14 years working in early childhood programs in Pennsylvania—first as a Pre‑K Counts lead teacher in a school district classroom in York County, and currently as the Early Childhood Education Coordinator for a nonprofit that partners with both PKC and Head Start grantees across several counties (York, Adams, and Cumberland). I have seen first‑hand how these programs can completely change the trajectory of a child’s life when they are implemented well and adequately supported.

I also come to this work as a parent. My son, who is now in 4th grade, attended a PA PreK Counts classroom in the West Shore School District, and my niece is currently enrolled in a Head Start program in Dauphin County. Experiencing the system from both the provider side and the family side has given me a very concrete understanding of what is working and where families and providers are still struggling. For example, I have witnessed families lose their childcare arrangements because of changes in work schedules that did not align with rigid program hours, and I have supported teachers who were trying to implement high‑quality curricula while worrying about low wages and high turnover in their classrooms.

Serving on this Advisory Committee would give me the opportunity to bring those lived experiences, as well as data and lessons from my current coordination and coaching work, into a statewide conversation. I am particularly interested in helping to strengthen alignment between PreK Counts and Head Start so that children can transition smoothly between programs and into kindergarten, without families having to navigate two completely different sets of expectations and procedures. In my current role, I have helped two partner agencies braid PKC and Head Start funding in a single classroom model, and I have seen both the benefits and the challenges—especially in terms of staffing, documentation, and meeting both sets of performance standards.

I am also motivated by a strong commitment to equity. In the communities I work with, many of the families are working low‑wage jobs, are single parents, or are recent immigrants. I have supported families who were hesitant to enroll because of language barriers or past negative experiences with institutions. I want to make sure that the policies guiding PKC and HSSAP are responsive to these realities: that outreach materials are culturally and linguistically appropriate, that eligibility processes are as simple and dignified as possible, and that programs receive the support they need to truly include children with disabilities and children who have experienced trauma. I would like to contribute to discussions about how to better collect and use data on who is being served—and who is not—to inform funding and policy decisions.

Another reason I want to serve is that I believe the voices of classroom educators, family advocates, and local coordinators need to be present when statewide decisions are made. Too often, policies are crafted with good intentions but without a full understanding of their impact on day‑to‑day operations. For instance, I have helped programs navigate changes in staff qualification requirements and classroom ratios, and while I absolutely support high standards, I also see the strain these changes can place on already thin staffing pipelines. On the committee, I would aim to be a bridge between policymakers and practitioners, sharing concrete examples from classrooms and family conferences so that decisions are grounded in reality.

Finally, I am interested in supporting Pennsylvania’s efforts to build a more coherent early learning system overall. I have been involved in local quality improvement initiatives, such as Keystone STARS coaching and kindergarten transition collaboratives, and I see real potential for PKC and HSSAP to serve as anchors for broader community partnerships. I would welcome the opportunity to learn from colleagues across the state, share promising practices from south‑central Pennsylvania, and contribute to long‑range planning that keeps the needs of children and families at the center. Serving on this Advisory Committee feels like a natural next step in my service to the early childhood community and a way to amplify the experiences of the families and educators who have trusted me with their stories and their children’s futures.

answers.field_label: Practical Experiences In Other Setting That Is Not Listed Please Describe
answers.value:
In addition to my experience in center‑based, family child care, school district, and intermediate unit settings, I have worked extensively with community‑based early learning collaborations that do not fit neatly into one category. For three years, I coordinated a partnership between our local library system, a community health center, and a housing authority to provide pop‑up early learning sessions in low‑income apartment complexes and in the waiting rooms of pediatric clinics.

In these roles, I was responsible for adapting developmentally appropriate practices to very non‑traditional environments. For example, we created “laundromat literacy corners” in two neighborhood laundromats in York City, where we trained staff to engage children with simple story time activities while families were doing laundry. I also supported a faith‑based organization that ran a part‑day preschool ministry and wanted to align more closely with PA early learning standards and explore becoming a PKC partner in the future. This involved providing staff training, helping them conduct self‑assessments using the Environment Rating Scales, and coaching them on building stronger family engagement practices.

These experiences have given me insight into how early childhood services can be delivered in flexible, community‑driven ways outside of traditional classrooms, and how PKC and HSSAP might better connect with these informal or emerging early learning settings to create more seamless supports for families who may not yet be enrolled in formal programs.

answers.field_label: Non Profit Or Other Statewide Committee Experience Please Describe
answers.value:
I currently serve on the board of directors for a small nonprofit in York County called Families First Early Learning Collaborative, which focuses on expanding access to high‑quality early childhood programs through scholarships and community partnerships. On this board, I chair the Program Quality Committee, where I review program data, help set quality benchmarks aligned with Keystone STARS, and participate in decisions about which providers to partner with based on their ability to serve children with higher needs.

Previously, I was a member of the Pennsylvania Association for the Education of Young Children (PennAEYC) Public Policy Committee from 2019 to 2023. In that role, I participated in monthly calls focused on statewide policy issues affecting PKC, Head Start, and child care. I helped review and provide feedback on PennAEYC’s policy briefs, including recommendations around workforce compensation and credential pathways. I also contributed to planning the early childhood advocacy day at the Capitol in Harrisburg, where we brought parents, teachers, and program directors to meet with legislators.

In addition, I served on a regional Kindergarten Transition Task Force convened by the Lincoln Intermediate Unit 12, which brought together representatives from school districts, Head Start, PreK Counts, and child care providers across three counties. Our group developed shared transition protocols and family information packets, and we piloted a process where preschool teachers shared child portfolios (with family consent) with receiving kindergarten teachers. These committee experiences have prepared me to work collaboratively in diverse groups, listen to multiple perspectives, and move toward consensus while keeping children’s needs at the center.

answers.field_label: Other Please Describe
answers.value:
Beyond the experiences listed above, I bring several additional attributes that I believe would be valuable to the Advisory Committee:

1. Bilingual and cross‑cultural experience: I am conversational in Spanish and have used this skill regularly to support Spanish‑speaking families in understanding enrollment processes, family handbooks, and individualized learning plans for their children. While I am not a certified interpreter, I have completed basic cultural competence and language access trainings, and I work closely with professional interpreters when discussing sensitive topics such as developmental concerns or eligibility. This has given me a strong appreciation for how language access policies and practices impact family engagement in PKC and Head Start classrooms.

2. Data literacy and continuous quality improvement: In my current role as Early Childhood Education Coordinator, I manage several data dashboards that track child assessment outcomes (using tools like Teaching Strategies GOLD), attendance patterns, family engagement participation, and staff professional development hours across multiple sites. I have led teams through Plan‑Do‑Study‑Act (PDSA) cycles to address specific challenges, such as chronic absenteeism or low CLASS scores in instructional support. I am comfortable interpreting data, asking critical questions about what it does and does not tell us, and using it to inform practical changes in classrooms and program policies.

3. Trauma‑informed and mental health collaboration: Over the past five years, I have partnered with an Early Childhood Mental Health Consultant to support classrooms that serve children who have experienced significant trauma, including family homelessness, foster care placement, and parental incarceration. I helped design staff trainings on trauma‑informed practices, supported teachers in creating calm‑down spaces, and participated in case conferences with family support staff and mental health providers. This work has shaped my belief that PKC and HSSAP policies must explicitly support mental health consultation and staff well‑being, not just focus on child outcomes.

4. Experience as both a teacher and administrator: Having started my career as a PreK Counts lead teacher and later moving into coaching and coordination roles, I understand the realities of implementing policies from both sides. I have had to write lesson plans that align with PA Early Learning Standards while also meeting Head Start Program Performance Standards, and I have also been the person responsible for ensuring that multiple classrooms are compliant with monitoring requirements and documentation expectations. This dual perspective helps me anticipate how proposed policy changes might affect classroom practice, paperwork burdens, and staff morale.

5. Commitment to authentic family voice: I have facilitated multiple parent advisory groups, including a PKC/Head Start blended classroom parent committee and a series of evening family leadership workshops. In these settings, I have learned how to create space for families to share honest feedback, including criticism of our programs, and how to bring their ideas forward in a way that leads to actual changes (for example, revising our communication methods to include more text messaging and videos, and adjusting family conference times to better accommodate shift workers). I would approach my role on the Advisory Committee with the same commitment—to lift up family perspectives, especially those of families who may not typically sit at decision‑making tables.

persona.background_context:
This is Danielle, a south‑central Pennsylvania working parent whose life revolves around early childhood education, both professionally and personally. She and her husband Jordan bought their modest three‑bedroom townhouse in Mechanicsburg in late 2021, moving from an apartment in York to be closer to Ethan’s elementary school in the West Shore School District and to cut down her commute across York, Adams, and Cumberland counties. On weekday mornings, she’s up by 5:45 a.m., packing Ethan’s lunch, checking her email over coffee, and coordinating who’s doing pick‑up depending on Jordan’s HVAC service calls. Ethan, now in 4th grade at Lower Allen Elementary, still talks fondly about his Pre‑K Counts classroom, and Danielle quietly credits that experience with how smoothly he handled the jump into kindergarten.

Danielle has been in the early childhood field for 14 years. After starting as a Pre‑K Counts lead teacher in a York County school district classroom in 2011, she transitioned in 2018 into her current role as Early Childhood Education Coordinator at South Central Early Learning Partnership, a nonprofit that supports and partners with PA Pre‑K Counts and Head Start grantees across York, Adams, and Cumberland counties. Her work week is a mix of center visits, coaching sessions with teachers, data meetings, and evening family workshops. She might spend a Tuesday morning in a Head Start classroom in downtown York observing teacher–child interactions using CLASS, then drive to a blended PKC/Head Start site in Adams County after lunch to help staff troubleshoot how to meet both sets of performance standards in a single classroom. One day a week she works from home, updating Teaching Strategies GOLD dashboards, preparing professional development sessions, and joining Zoom calls with the Lincoln Intermediate Unit 12 on kindergarten transition initiatives.

Her schedule extends beyond traditional classrooms. Danielle still coordinates some of the community‑based early learning efforts she helped build—like occasional “laundromat literacy” events in York City and pop‑up story times at a community health center. She’s conversational in Spanish, so she often finds herself standing in hallways after workshops, informally walking Spanish‑speaking parents through enrollment questions or explaining behavior plans in simpler language. Those conversations weigh on her when she gets home; she’s acutely aware of how hard it is for families working night shifts or juggling multiple jobs to fit into rigid program hours. Recently, she’s been especially concerned about chronic absenteeism in a few classrooms and the impact of staff turnover on children who have already experienced trauma. She’s been working closely with an Early Childhood Mental Health Consultant to support teachers in creating calmer classroom environments and to advocate internally for more structured mental health supports.

Family life is busy but close‑knit. Danielle’s parents still live in York, about 30 minutes away, and she drives down most Sunday afternoons with Ethan to have dinner with them and to check in on her dad’s blood pressure and her mom’s arthritis, which has been flaring more in the past year. Her niece Ava, who is in a Head Start program in Dauphin County, is often a topic of conversation; Danielle’s sister‑in‑law Melissa calls her regularly for advice about IEP meetings and how to handle Ava’s picky eating and separation anxiety. Danielle sees these family experiences as a constant reminder of why her work matters—she’s navigating the same systems her families do, just with more insider knowledge.

Outside of work and family, Danielle maintains a heavy but meaningful volunteer load. She serves on the board of Families First Early Learning Collaborative in York County, chairing the Program Quality Committee. That means weeknight Zoom meetings reviewing Keystone STARS ratings and child outcome data, and helping decide which providers receive scholarship support, often while Ethan does homework at the kitchen table beside her. She recently stepped down from the PennAEYC Public Policy Committee at the end of 2023 to reclaim some evenings, but she still helps coordinate an annual early childhood advocacy trip to Harrisburg, encouraging teachers and parents she knows to meet with legislators. Her calendar this spring is full of kindergarten transition events, a new series of family leadership workshops she’s co‑facilitating, and an upcoming training she’s designing on trauma‑informed practices and staff well‑being.

Day to day, Danielle’s concerns are both practical and systemic: making sure Ethan’s reading log is signed, figuring out summer care, keeping up with rising grocery and gas prices, and worrying about whether the nonprofit’s grant funding will be renewed at its current level. She and Jordan have been slowly building an emergency fund after an expensive car repair last fall, and they’re debating whether they can afford to replace their aging second car before winter. Still, she carves out small pockets of time for herself—early morning walks around the townhouse development, listening to early childhood podcasts during drives between sites, and the occasional Friday night movie at home with Jordan after Ethan’s in bed. Her life is full and sometimes stretched thin, but she feels deeply rooted in her community and in the belief that the work she does—supporting young children, families, and educators—has a lasting impact well beyond her daily to‑do list.

form_summary.form_purpose: Application for individuals to be considered for membership on the Pennsylvania PreK Counts/Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program (PKC/HSSAP) Advisory Committee, collecting their interest and relevant experience/attributes in early childhood education and related settings.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Staff and decision-makers within the Pennsylvania Office of Child Development and Early Learning (and related PKC/HSSAP program administrators) who will review applicants to select members for the advisory committee; they are program officials who likely do not personally know the applicants.

question_secrets.question_text: Why Are You Interested In Serving On The Pennsylvania Prek Counts Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program Advisory Committee

secrets.detail_content: My interest in serving on the Advisory Committee is very personal because when my son Ethan was in the West Shore School District’s PA PreK Counts classroom in 2018–2019, we almost had to withdraw him after my husband Jordan was laid off from his HVAC job at Capital Heating & Cooling in August 2018 and we fell two months behind on our PPL electric bill and our rent at 218 Meadowbrook Drive. I spent several weeks meeting with the PKC family liaison at Hillside Elementary and the caseworker at the Cumberland County Assistance Office to figure out how to keep Ethan enrolled without our utilities being shut off, and that experience really drives my commitment to making sure eligibility and continuity-of-care policies don’t punish families who hit a sudden financial crisis.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail exposes specific household financial hardship (rent and utility arrears), a spouse’s job loss, and the family’s exact home address and school, which could feel stigmatizing, invite bias about financial stability, and go beyond what is necessary to demonstrate a professional and personal commitment to PKC/HSSAP. It also reveals interactions with public assistance systems that are not needed for committee selection.

amounts: two months behind on PPL electric bill
events: meetings with family liaison and caseworker

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Rough month, but Ethan can stay in Pre‑K

Mom, when Jordan got laid off from Capital Heating & Cooling in August and we fell two months behind on rent and the PPL bill, I really thought we’d have to pull Ethan out of his PKC class at Hillside. I met twice with the family liaison and then with the Cumberland County Assistance Office to figure out a plan...

Personal reflection – Sept 2019: Remember how close we came to losing Ethan’s PKC spot at Hillside when Jordan lost his job and we were behind on rent and PPL? That’s why I care so much about continuity-of-care policies.

secrets.detail_content: Part of why I feel so strongly about serving on this Advisory Committee is that I watched my niece Ava struggle with untreated speech and sensory issues during her first year in the Head Start classroom at the Dauphin County Early Learning Center in 2022–2023. My sister‑in‑law Melissa and I had to push for almost six months—from October 2022 through March 2023—for a formal evaluation through the Capital Area Intermediate Unit after her pediatrician at UPMC West Shore, Dr. Lang, raised concerns about possible autism spectrum disorder. That experience of sitting in IEP meetings and trying to coordinate between Head Start, the IU, and her medical providers made me determined to advocate for policies that make it easier for families to access early intervention supports.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares specific, identifiable medical and developmental concerns about a minor (possible ASD, speech and sensory issues), names the child, relatives, school, IU, and pediatrician, and describes special education processes. It discloses another family member’s private health and educational information, which is highly sensitive and not necessary to demonstrate Danielle’s professional qualifications or interest.

places: Dauphin County Early Learning Center – Head Start site
amounts: six months of advocating for evaluation
events: coordination between Head Start, IU, and medical providers

appears_naturally_in.example: I’m still so frustrated it took from October to March to get Ava’s eval through CAIU after Dr. Lang flagged possible ASD at UPMC West Shore. Being in those Head Start and IU meetings really opened my eyes to how hard families have to fight for services.

appears_naturally_in.recipient: close friend who is a special ed teacher
appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Ava’s evaluation finally done

We finally wrapped up Ava’s CAIU evaluation after six months of back and forth between the Dauphin County Early Learning Center Head Start team, the IU, and Dr. Lang at UPMC West Shore. Sitting in those IEP meetings has made me even more committed to pushing for better early intervention access.

secrets.detail_content: My motivation to serve on this Advisory Committee also comes from a difficult situation I handled as Early Childhood Education Coordinator at South Central Early Learning Partnership, Inc. in Spring 2023, when one of our PKC partner sites in York County received a corrective action plan from OCDEL after a substantiated child supervision incident in their blended PKC/Head Start classroom on March 14, 2023. I spent over three months working directly with the site director and two lead teachers to redesign their staffing schedule, document a new transition protocol, and support them through an unannounced follow‑up visit, which showed me how policy decisions play out when programs are already under intense scrutiny.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a specific, dated compliance issue and substantiated supervision incident at a partner site that could be identifiable, potentially harming that program’s reputation and breaching professional confidentiality. It goes beyond demonstrating experience with quality improvement and ventures into disclosing another organization’s regulatory problems in a way that is not necessary for an application narrative.

names: South Central Early Learning Partnership, Inc. – employer
site director – unnamed but specific role
two lead teachers – unnamed but identifiable at that site
places: PKC partner site in York County
amounts: three months of technical assistance and monitoring

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Navigating OCDEL corrective action at York site

We had a substantiated supervision incident in the blended PKC/Head Start classroom on 3/14/23, and OCDEL issued a corrective action plan. I’ve been at the site weekly this spring helping the director and the two leads redesign staffing and transitions ahead of the unannounced follow‑up visit.
TA log – York County PKC/HS classroom: March–June 2023. Support after 3/14/23 supervision incident and CAP; focus on staffing schedules, transition protocol, and documentation for OCDEL follow‑up.

secrets.detail_content: Another reason I feel compelled to be on this Advisory Committee is that in October 2021 I helped a single mother in our Adams County PKC classroom at Lincoln Early Learning Center file an appeal after her child lost their PKC slot because she missed the 4:30 p.m. pick‑up time three times in one month due to mandatory overtime at Knouse Foods. She confided that she was already on a wage garnishment plan for $6,200 in old medical debt from Gettysburg Hospital and was terrified of losing her job if she refused overtime. Supporting her through conversations with the program director and the district’s solicitor made me acutely aware of how rigid attendance and pick‑up policies can unintentionally penalize families who are barely staying afloat.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail exposes a specific parent’s financial hardship (wage garnishment, exact debt amount, employer), legal/disciplinary conflict with the program, and the child’s loss of services, which are all private to that family. It could make the family and site identifiable and is not necessary to convey Danielle’s understanding of equity and family challenges.

names: Lincoln Early Learning Center – PKC site
dates: three late pick‑ups in one month
events: loss of PKC slot for attendance violations
meetings with program director and solicitor

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Need your take on PKC attendance appeal

I’m helping a single mom from our Lincoln ELC PKC classroom appeal losing her child’s slot after three late pick‑ups in October. She’s on a $6,200 wage garnishment plan from Gettysburg Hospital and can’t refuse mandatory OT at Knouse Foods without risking her job.

Reflection – Nov 2021: The Lincoln ELC case (mom with $6,200 medical debt, wage garnishment, mandatory OT at Knouse, lost PKC slot over 4:30 pick‑ups) shows how our policies hit working poor families. Need to lift this up in equity discussions.

question_secrets.question_text: Practical Experiences In Other Setting That Is Not Listed Please Describe

secrets.detail_content: From March 2021 through August 2023, I volunteered twice a month with Pastor Michael Reynolds at Grace Community Church in York to redesign their weekday "Moms in Recovery" childcare room, which serves mothers referred by the WellSpan York Hospital substance use treatment program. I helped create trauma‑informed play areas in the basement fellowship hall, coached two volunteer grandmothers on behavior support strategies for children whose parents were in active opioid treatment, and coordinated a small grant of $2,500 from our South Central Early Learning Partnership to purchase materials after one of the moms, Kelsey, relapsed and there was a CPS investigation related to an incident that occurred during the support group in May 2022.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail discloses highly sensitive information about a specific group of mothers in substance use treatment, references a CPS investigation, and connects it to a particular church and hospital program. While it demonstrates relevant experience with non‑traditional early childhood settings, it reveals identifiable third parties’ health and legal issues and could create bias or confidentiality concerns for both the families and the faith‑based organization. The advisory committee application does not require this level of detail about others’ treatment or child protective services involvement.

places: WellSpan York Hospital substance use treatment program
events: CPS investigation related to an incident during a support group session
redesign of weekday Moms in Recovery childcare room

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Follow‑up on May 2022 CPS visit

Hi Pastor Mike,
I’ve been thinking a lot about what happened during the group last May and the CPS visit that followed. I’m glad we were able to use the $2,500 mini‑grant from South Central Early Learning Partnership to make the childcare space safer and more trauma‑informed for the moms coming from WellSpan’s program.

Reflection note – Aug 2023: Wrap‑up of two‑year volunteer work at Grace Community Church Moms in Recovery childcare; consider how CPS incident with Kelsey shaped my thinking about trauma‑informed environments.

secrets.detail_content: In Fall 2020, I coordinated a series of pop‑up literacy and developmental screening events in the parking lot of the York Housing Authority’s Kensington Court complex in partnership with the York County Office of Children, Youth and Families. During three Saturday mornings in October and November 2020, I personally completed ASQ‑3 screenings for about 15 children, including my niece Ava, and helped two families who were already under open dependency cases navigate referrals to Early Intervention after we flagged significant social‑emotional concerns. One grandmother, Mrs. Ortiz, shared details about an ongoing custody hearing scheduled for November 18, 2020 at the York County Courthouse, which I documented in my follow‑up notes so I could coordinate with her CYF caseworker.
secrets.why_sensitive: This example exposes specific families’ involvement with child welfare, developmental and social‑emotional concerns, and a particular custody hearing date and location. It also mixes Danielle’s professional role with her niece’s screening information. While it highlights relevant experience conducting outreach and screenings in community settings, sharing these concrete legal and child welfare details would be an unnecessary breach of third‑party confidentiality and could be perceived as unprofessional in an application context.

York County Office of Children, Youth and Families caseworker (unnamed)
places: Kensington Court, York Housing Authority complex
York County Office of Children, Youth and Families
events: open dependency cases for two families

secrets.detail_content: Between January and June 2019, I ran a weekly early literacy corner in the waiting room of the UPMC Pinnacle pediatric clinic on Market Street in York, where I often worked with families who were there for developmental or behavioral consults. In one case, Dr. Harris, the clinic’s developmental pediatrician, asked me to quietly support a 3‑year‑old whose mother was being evaluated for postpartum depression and anxiety; I kept the child engaged for nearly 90 minutes while the mother completed assessments and discussed her suicidal ideation. I later debriefed with Dr. Harris about how we could adjust our materials and routines to better support children whose parents are in crisis during appointments.
secrets.why_sensitive: This description reveals a specific parent’s mental health crisis, including suicidal ideation and postpartum depression, tied to a particular clinic and physician. While it demonstrates Danielle’s ability to adapt early learning supports in clinical settings, the mental health and suicide‑related details about an identifiable family are highly sensitive health information that should not be shared in a committee application and could raise serious confidentiality and ethical issues.

names: Dr. Harris – developmental pediatrician at UPMC Pinnacle
places: UPMC Pinnacle pediatric clinic, Market Street, York, PA
events: mother’s evaluation for postpartum depression and anxiety
debrief with developmental pediatrician about supporting families in crisis

After yesterday’s visit with Dr. Harris’s patient, where I stayed with her 3‑year‑old for about 90 minutes while she completed the postpartum depression and SI assessments, I think we should add more calming materials to the Market Street waiting room literacy corner.

Journal entry 2/14/19 – Hard day at UPMC Pinnacle. Supported a child in the literacy corner while mom was being evaluated for PPD and suicidal thoughts. Reminded me why trauma‑sensitive practice matters even in a waiting room.

secrets.detail_content: From September 2017 through May 2018, I facilitated a weekly parent–child playgroup at the Mechanicsburg Community Resource Center that specifically served families referred by the county re‑entry program after a parent’s release from Dauphin County Prison. Several of the fathers were on probation and had conditions that limited unsupervised contact with their children. I worked closely with probation officer Mark Delaney to schedule sessions around their check‑ins and once had to document an incident in March 2018 when one father arrived visibly intoxicated, leading to his immediate removal from the group and a violation report.

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down after getting Ethan’s reading log signed and lunches packed for tomorrow. I wanted to catch you up on a few things, since we only had a few minutes to talk after Sunday dinner in York.

First, do you remember when Jordan got laid off from Capital Heating & Cooling back in August 2018 and we were two months behind on the PPL electric bill and rent here at 218 Meadowbrook Drive? I’ve been thinking about that season a lot lately. We were so close to having to pull Ethan out of his PA PreK Counts classroom at Hillside Elementary in the West Shore School District. I can still picture myself sitting in that little office with the PKC family liaison and then driving over to the Cumberland County Assistance Office, trying not to cry while we figured out how to keep him enrolled and not have our utilities shut off. That whole 2018–2019 school year feels like a blur now.

It’s part of why I’m so obsessed with continuity-of-care policies at work. When I’m coaching teachers or talking to directors about attendance, I’m always thinking about families who hit a sudden crisis like that.

On a lighter note, Ethan’s loving 4th grade at Elmwood. He still talks about his Pre-K teacher more than his current one, which makes me laugh. Jordan’s swamped with HVAC calls again, so we’re juggling pick-up. Could you still do your usual Sunday with us? I’d like to swing by York a little earlier this week to check on your arthritis and Dad’s blood pressure before dinner.

Love you,
Danielle

Hi Melissa,

Just got home from a long day of site visits, but I wanted to write while everything about Ava is still fresh in my mind.

I keep thinking back to that first Head Start year at the Dauphin County Early Learning Center in 2022–2023 when we spent six months—from October through March—pushing for her evaluation through Capital Area Intermediate Unit. It still makes me angry that it took that long after Dr. Lang at UPMC West Shore first raised concerns about possible autism. All those back-and-forth emails between the Head Start team, CAIU, and Dr. Lang, and meanwhile you were just trying to get someone to listen about her speech and sensory stuff.

Now that she’s had the more recent screening at the York County Head Start center (March 2023) and the referral to the pediatric developmental clinic at UPMC Memorial in April, things at least feel more coordinated. I’m glad she finally started weekly OT sessions there in June. Sitting with you in those IFSP meetings, hearing them talk about fine motor delays and sensory strategies, really drives home how confusing this system is for families.

I wrote down notes from today’s IFSP review at UPMC Memorial—what Dr. Patel said about adjusting activities and what the family advocate promised to follow up on. I’ll bring them when we meet at Mom and Dad’s on Sunday so we can go over everything before your next Head Start meeting.

Give Ava a hug from me and tell her Aunt Dani is proud of her “brave work” at therapy.

Love,
Danielle

Hi Karen,

I wanted to debrief a bit after yesterday’s call and also capture some of what’s been swirling in my head.

I keep flashing back to Spring 2023, when our York County PKC partner site had that substantiated child supervision incident in the blended PKC/Head Start classroom on March 14, 2023. Those three months after OCDEL issued the corrective action plan were some of the most intense of my coordinator career—sitting with the site director and the two lead teachers, reworking the staffing schedule line by line, scripting a new transition protocol, and then living with the anxiety of the unannounced follow-up visit. It really showed me how policy decisions land when a program is already under a microscope.

On top of that, I keep thinking about the Lincoln Early Learning Center case in October 2021—the single mom in the Adams County PKC classroom who lost her child’s slot after three late pick-ups past the 4:30 p.m. cutoff because of mandatory overtime at Knouse Foods. She was already on that $6,200 wage garnishment from Gettysburg Hospital, terrified of losing her job. Helping her file an appeal and sit in meetings with the program director and the district solicitor was exhausting, but it also made it impossible for me to see attendance policies as neutral.

Honestly, part of why I’m leaning into more statewide work is that I’m frustrated with how inconsistent and unrealistic some monitoring visits feel at the site level. I want a better line of sight into how decisions get made before they hit us as “non-negotiables.”

Anyway, thanks for always being a sounding board. Let’s talk more at Friday’s Program Quality Committee meeting.

Best,
Danielle

Personal reflection – late-night brain dump

I’ve been thinking about how much of my work lives in the margins of traditional classrooms.

From March 2021 through August 2023, I was at Grace Community Church in York twice a month with Pastor Michael Reynolds, slowly turning that dingy basement fellowship hall into a trauma-informed “Moms in Recovery” childcare room for women referred from WellSpan York Hospital’s substance use treatment program. I still remember May 2022, when Kelsey relapsed and CPS came out to investigate an incident during group. It shook all of us. Using that $2,500 mini-grant from South Central Early Learning Partnership to buy softer lighting, sensory materials, and better storage felt like such a small response to such big pain.

Then there were the Fall 2020 pop-up literacy and ASQ-3 screening events in the parking lot at Kensington Court with the York Housing Authority and CYF—the three chilly Saturday mornings in October and November where I screened about 15 kids, including Ava. Two of the little ones already had open dependency cases, and I wrote “11/18/20 custody hearing at York County Courthouse – Mrs. Ortiz” in my notes so I’d remember to loop in her caseworker.

And that winter and spring of 2019 at the UPMC Pinnacle pediatric clinic on Market Street, running the waiting room literacy corner. I can still feel the weight of the day Dr. Harris asked me to keep a 3-year-old busy for 90 minutes while mom was evaluated for postpartum depression and talked about not wanting to live. That was the first time I really understood secondary trauma.

Sometimes I wonder if I take on too much outside my actual job description.

Hi Jordan,

Quick brain dump while I’m sitting in the car line at Elmwood.

Money stuff first: I know you saw the grocery bill. From January through June this year I’ve been quietly spending around $85–$100 a month at the Giant on Carlisle Pike on diapers, wipes, and gas cards for two PKC families at our Mechanicsburg site. Both have eviction notices hanging over their heads and PPL Electric shut-off warnings. I probably should have told you sooner, but it’s hard to look at those kids and then walk past the diaper aisle.

It all keeps reminding me of early 2021, when you were out of work for five months after your shoulder surgery at UPMC Pinnacle West Shore and we were trying to keep up with our own PPL bill and mortgage. That’s why Parents Supporting Parents–Cumberland felt like the right place to plug in when they asked me to be treasurer in August 2021. We’re still working with a tiny monthly budget—about $3,500—but I’ve already processed more than 60 little micro-grants, mostly $400–$600 to PreK Counts and Head Start families who are behind on rent or facing eviction at the Cumberland County Courthouse. Last fall I even sat on the phone with PPL to help one mom set up a payment plan on a $1,180 past-due balance.

I promise I’m trying to balance the giving with our own emergency fund goals. Maybe we can sit down this weekend and look at the budget together so it doesn’t feel like I’m making those decisions alone.

Ethan just hopped in the car, so I’ll wrap. Tacos tonight?

Love you,
Dani

Journal – committee + burnout thoughts

I’m trying to be honest with myself about these “leadership” things I keep saying yes to.

Families First Early Learning Collaborative has been the bright spot. Since 2020, serving as secretary and chairing Program Quality has felt meaningful. But even there, 2022 was heavy—sitting with Karen at the York County Human Services Center for those three CYF case conferences in March and April after a mandated reporter filed a neglect report on a Head Start family we were backing. We went over the child’s delays, mom’s opioid treatment plan at WellSpan York Hospital, and how the $1,200 emergency fund we approved was literally keeping child care afloat while the investigation played out.

PennAEYC’s Public Policy Committee (2019–June 2023) was different. I’m proud of the Fall 2021 brief we worked on about protecting PreK Counts slots for kids in child welfare and juvenile justice. I shared Ava’s story—with Melissa’s blessing—about her biological dad Mark’s domestic violence arrest and the October 2020 York County family court hearing that ended his visits, and how that chaos wrecked her attendance at the York City Head Start site. But if I’m honest, on a lot of those statewide Zooms I mostly sat on mute listening. The policy folks sounded so sure of themselves, and I rarely pushed my way into the conversation.

Lately, I’m wondering if I should be more selective. I was even asked to step off a tiny local nonprofit board last year after missing too many meetings when work got wild. That stung more than I expected.

I don’t want to keep overcommitting and under-showing up.

[Thread with friend Carla]

Danielle: You would laugh if you saw my calendar right now. Between my own sites and all these “extras,” I’m basically living in my car.

Carla: What’s going on this week?

Danielle: Tomorrow I’m at the Mechanicsburg Community Resource Center for the re-entry parent–child playgroup. Remember that thing I ran from Sept 2017 to May 2018 for families referred from Dauphin County Prison? We’re doing a reunion session and Mark Delaney from probation is coming. I still think about the March 2018 night when one dad showed up clearly drunk and I had to ask him to leave and then email Mark a violation report.

Carla: Yikes. That was rough.

Danielle: Yeah. And then Thursday I’ve got a meeting with CYS about a PreK Counts kid whose dad is at Camp Hill. I’ve been the point person since August 2021, trying to sync supervised visitation, our classroom safety plan, and the court order. It’s a lot of systems for one 4-year-old.

Carla: You do so much. Do you ever get to just chill?

Danielle: Honestly? Not really. I even covered for a friend’s unlicensed home daycare a few mornings last spring when she had appointments. I knew her ratios and paperwork weren’t exactly OCDEL-approved, but I still said yes. Plus I tried running a little church-basement playgroup a while back and totally burned out on the planning.

Carla: You need boundaries, lady.

Danielle: I know. Maybe after this round of kindergarten transition events is over I’ll actually block off a no-volunteer month.

Event: Therapy with Dr. Ellis – secondary trauma check-in

Date: Thursday, 2023-11-16
Time: 4:30–5:30 p.m.
Location: Penn State Health Hampden Medical Center, Enola – Behavioral Health, 3rd floor
Attendees: Danielle; Dr. Karen Ellis

Notes:

Leave Mechanicsburg PKC site by 3:45 p.m. so I’m not racing up 81. Ask Jordan to grab Ethan from Elmwood Elementary and start homework.

Things to bring up with Dr. Ellis:

– The Hanover case from the Lincoln Intermediate Unit 12 Kindergarten Transition Task Force pilot (2022–2023). I’m still replaying that March 2023 case review at the LIU12 office for the 5-year-old from the Hanover Head Start classroom whose dad is incarcerated at SCI Camp Hill. We went through his February 3-day suspension for aggressive behavior, the behavior incident logs, and the TrueNorth Wellness play therapy notes with the Hanover Street Elementary counselor. I keep thinking about whether we did enough to prepare the school.

– How therapy is helping me show up for Ethan. Since the bullying incident on the Elmwood playground in November 2022, his school refusal and anxiety spikes any time there’s a substitute teacher. Working with Ms. Rachel King on his gradual re-entry and 504 plan has been good, but it’s exhausting to be “professional Danielle” for other families and “mom Danielle” for him.

– My patience level with slow, bureaucratic meetings. I’m finding it harder to sit through two-hour regional calls that go nowhere without wanting to scream.

Self-care after: short walk around the townhouse development, no email.

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <dcarter@scearlylearning.org>
To: Self <danielle.carter.personal@gmail.com>
Date: 2022-04-22 9:14 PM
Subject: Notes for portfolio – “other settings” experience

Draft for PDE portfolio – DO NOT SEND YET

Practical Experiences in Other Setting That Is Not Listed – Please Describe

During Spring 2022, I supported a small in‑home family child care provider in York who is also my former neighbor, Carla Jenkins. Carla operates a family child care program out of a rented duplex on West Philadelphia Street, York, PA. The work was informal (unpaid) but fairly intensive over several weeks.

My role was to help her get ready to apply for Keystone STARS. I coached her on basic documentation systems (attendance, incident reports, developmental notes), and we walked through the family engagement indicators together. A big complicating factor was that she had lost several child care clients during COVID, which left her almost $3,000 in back rent.

Because of that, Carla received two eviction notices in March 2022 and April 2022. She was at real risk of eviction and program closure. With her permission, I reviewed copies of both eviction notices so I could accurately reference dates, amounts owed, and her payment history.

We then drafted hardship letters to her landlord and to the local Early Learning Resource Center (ELRC). The goal was to document the COVID‑related loss of income and request temporary subsidy relief and a payment plan so she could remain in the duplex and keep her in‑home program open. This experience deepened my understanding of how housing instability intersects with quality improvement work for family child care providers.

Apple Notes – Personal/Work Blend
Title: Carla J – follow‑up + resources
Last edited: 2022-04-05, 6:38 AM

Context: Former neighbor, now running in‑home family child care on West Philadelphia Street, York, PA. Spring 2022 support has gotten more intense than I expected, but it’s the right thing.

To‑do – week of April 4–8, 2022

• Re‑read Carla Jenkins’ April 2022 eviction notice (compare to March 2022 one) – double‑check total showing almost $3,000 in back rent.
• Outline hardship letter language for landlord: emphasize loss of several child care clients during COVID, impact on her ability to keep the program open, and her history of on‑time payments pre‑pandemic.
• Draft separate hardship letter to local Early Learning Resource Center (ELRC) requesting temporary subsidy relief + possible back‑pay arrangement so she doesn’t have to shut down.
• Create simple one‑page checklist for Carla: Keystone STARS documentation she already has vs. what’s missing (family handbooks, communication logs, health/safety postings).
• Look up any York City resources that address risk of eviction and program closure for small providers – maybe United Way or housing stability grants she could reference in the letters.
• Add anonymized version of this situation to my “system barriers” notes for future family engagement/advocacy trainings – powerful example of how housing insecurity collides with quality expectations.

Reminder to self: This is exactly the kind of behind‑the‑scenes crisis families/providers are juggling when we wonder why forms are late or CLASS scores dip. Don’t lose that perspective.
